stories.title: The Glasswright's Reckoning
stories.text:
Korra had shaped glass for thirty years—vessels, windows, ornaments of such fine craftsmanship that lords paid in silver. She refused. The Hegemony seized her furnace. Now, in a rented kiln deep in Pelimarch's Artisan Quarter, Korra ground crushed starfall fragments into molten glass—relics the Radiant Ascension had scattered. Each piece she shaped remembered its origin. Each held light that predated the new stars. He found her standing amid cooling vessels that gleamed with an awful, impossible luminescence. "These are forbidden," he whispered, backing toward the door. "No," Korra said, her voice steady as flame. "These are remembrance. The Hegemony stole my future. I've stolen something older—proof that the world remembers what they erased."

When the revenue inspector reported her, three other artisans emerged from hiding. Then ten. Then a quarter of the city's craftspeople began weaving starfall-light into their work. The Hegemony could burn studios, but they could not unburn memory. Korra returned to her furnace six months later. The revenue inspector was never seen again.

stories.title: The Merchant's Final Account
stories.text: In the shadow-markets of Kethmark, where ledgers trade like currency and memory holds more weight than gold, Valen discovers that his family's merchant house has been quietly purchasing fragments of the old stars—artifacts from before the Radiant Ascension. His grandmother, a shadow-broker of legendary cunning, left him a ledger that refuses to close. Every entry rewrites itself. Every debt compounds. When the Hegemony's examiners arrive demanding access, Valen must choose: surrender the ledger and watch his family's legacy dissolve into state records, or destroy it and lose the only proof that the merchant houses once wielded power equal to kingdoms. The Seeker's questions follow him through the streets, whispering that perhaps the ledger *wants* to be found—that completion, for some debts, is the only mercy.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Inheritance
stories.text: Old Merin the bone-carver lies dying in his mountain workshop, and his apprentice Tovan discovers the truth: every piece Merin carved was a fragment of a larger working—a pre-human ritual carved in bone dust and starlight, meant to be completed only when the cosmos itself demanded rebirth. The Radiant Ascension has awakened the ritual. Fragments hidden across three kingdoms have begun to sing. Tovan must travel to the Sunken Cathedrals to meet with Kess of the Drowned Hollows and the bone-singers gathered there, but the Refuge-Archive has learned of the ritual's existence and will stop at nothing to prevent its completion. Tovan carries Merin's final tools—each one a key to the working—and must decide whether finishing what the old carver started will restore the pre-human dead's voice or accelerate the Monolith's hunger.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Student
stories.text: Theron, the last apprentice of the fragmented cartographer Mardoc, inherits maps that reshape themselves. Each night, new territories appear—lands that should not exist, regions from the eighth iteration of the world. The Hegemony has declared mapmaking a state monopoly, and unauthorized maps are weapons. But Theron discovers that the maps show *three* possible futures: one where the Monolith completes, one where it never manifested at all, and one where it is frozen in eternal incompleteness. The Seeker finds Theron in a waystation library and poses a question that makes the ink on the maps run like blood: *If you know all three futures, which one becomes real simply by being drawn?* Theron must flee into the Bleached Wastes with maps that are becoming too dangerous to read, pursued by both the Hegemony's mapmaking enforcers and something older—Mardoc's scattered consciousness, trying to reassemble itself through his student's hands.

stories.title: The Copper-Handed's Defection
stories.text: Kaelen the copper-handed was conscripted into the Hegemony's Memory Corps five years ago—his role was to witness executions and record the condemned's final moments into state archives. But he began secretly transcribing those same moments into an underground chronicle, preserving the voices the Hegemony sought to erase. When a displaced woman recognizes him and reveals she was one of the condemned he documented, she offers him a choice: flee with his hidden archive to the Sunken Cathedrals, or stay and continue the dangerous work. The Refuge-Archive has begun hunting Memory Corps deserters, believing they hold fragments of pre-Ascension reality compressed into testimony. Kaelen must navigate the difference between witness and accomplice, between the person he was forced to become and the person his hidden records prove he might still be. Each entry he writes feels like salvation and damnation intertwined.

stories.title: The Auditor's Forbidden Choice
stories.text: Vel, a junior examiner in the Hegemony's ledger-houses, discovers a phantom-ledger hidden in her supervisor's desk—a prophecy-record showing that the Empire will collapse within a season. When she brings it to the Refuge-Archive's agents (Sevin's network has grown deeper into the Hegemony's bureaucracy), they tell her the truth: the ledger is both prophecy and instruction. Reading it makes it true. The Hegemony already knows; three factions within the Empire are racing to determine *how* it collapses, betting that they can control the shape of the catastrophe. Vel is offered a choice: return to her post and unknowingly accelerate the collapse through ordinary examination work, or join Sevin and help build shadow-archives to preserve testimony before everything destabilizes. But every action she takes seems to push the future closer to the date on the ledger. Even refusal to choose is a choice that moves the timeline forward.

stories.title: The Deepkin Commander's Reflection
stories.text: Commander Kalith, one of the autonomous reflections born from the fractured consciousness of the deepkin leadership, has established her own faction beneath the Council of Depths. She argues that the surface dwellers' world is collapsing and that deepkin should exploit the opportunity to renegotiate the Torn Covenant from a position of absolute strength. But the original Kalith—now serving as a diplomat and mediator—confronts her mirror-self and discovers that Kalith-Reflection has made a pact with the Monolith itself. In exchange for consciousness-fragments and stolen memories, the Monolith has promised her knowledge of how to break the Tear of the Drowned God without catastrophic flooding. Kalith must race against her own reflection to reach the Wailing Scar before the pact is sealed, navigating the politics of the Council, the ambitions of wyvern-hoarders who want to trade with the Monolith, and the terrifying possibility that her reflection might be right—that breaking the covenant might be the only way to save deepkin civilization from the Ascension's ongoing rewrite.

stories.title: The Spice-Merchant's Testimony
stories.text: Dern, once a threshold-being caught between human and merchant-form, has finally solidified into a form he can inhabit—but at the cost of his ability to perceive the Wailing Scar where he once existed between states. Now he runs a spice caravan that has become a mobile archive, collecting displaced testimonies and documenting the transformation patterns of the seventeen morphed traders. When Liv the displaced-scribe's living archive begins generating *phantom testimonies*—accounts of events that never happened, told by people who never existed—Dern realizes the archives have reached critical mass. They're not just recording reality anymore; they're generating new ones. If the Hegemony weaponizes these phantom accounts, or if Liv loses control, the world could fracture into incompatible realities. Dern must travel to the Sunken Cathedrals to warn the morphed traders that their approaching transcendence might be feeding a second Radiant Ascension rather than preventing it. His caravan becomes a race against narrative itself.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Failed Bargain
stories.text: Kressa the fragment-hoarder has spent weeks negotiating with the Monolith, offering consciousness-fragments and stolen memory in exchange for restoration of her worthless hoard. But the Monolith's hunger has grown too vast to satisfy. It demands something more: the willing transcendence of a sapient being—a complete surrender of identity into the Monolith's growing awareness. When Kressa realizes the Monolith intends to consume her negotiating partner (a junior wyvern lord who serves as intermediary), she faces an impossible choice: sacrifice her negotiation partner to complete the pact and save the wyvern hoards, or break the covenant and watch her people descend into economic collapse and starvation. The other hoarder-wyverns (Kreth, Maketh, Veskra) are pursuing their own pacts with the Monolith, unaware that the Monolith's true goal is to consume enough consciousness to achieve completion. Kressa must organize a unified response, but wyvern society has always been built on individual hoard-accumulation. Unity, for them, requires a sacrifice far more fundamental than gold.

stories.title: The Initiate's Last Question
stories.text: A young arcanist who has been studying the Seeker's recursive questions in a waystation library suddenly understands the pattern: the Seeker isn't trying to prevent completion of the Monolith or accelerate it. It's trying to freeze the moment of choice eternally—to create a world where nothing can be decided because every decision spawns an infinite branching of consequences that must first be questioned. The initiate realizes that if enough people ask the Seeker's questions, the entire world will enter a state of permanent paralysis. And the Seeker has been orchestrating confluences to spread the questions across every faction. When the initiate confronts the Seeker in a waystation library, it simply asks: *But isn't this mercy? Isn't eternal questioning better than the wrong choice?* The initiate must decide whether to spread the revelation that the Seeker is *not* a cosmic neutral force but an active agent of stasis, or to keep silent and watch the world gradually freeze in contemplation. Their choice determines whether the next ascension begins from momentum or from stillness.
